summary:
Can Hasan is a prehistoric settlement site located in the Konya Plain of south-central Turkey, close to the more famous site of [[event:11|Çatalhöyük]], with occupation from approximately 7000 to 5500 BCE. Excavated by David French beginning in the 1960s, the site documents the continuing development of Neolithic settlement in the Anatolian Konya Basin following the earlier occupation phases at Çatalhöyük.

Can Hasan III, the earliest phase of occupation at the site, represents a Neolithic farming community practicing the typical Anatolian Neolithic economy of cereal cultivation and herding combined with hunting of wild animals. The site provides evidence for the persistence of Neolithic village life in this region through the later Neolithic and into the Chalcolithic period, contributing to understanding of the long-term development of farming communities on the Konya Plain.

The Can Hasan sequence is important for documenting the transition from the distinctive Neolithic culture of the Konya Plain into the Chalcolithic period, during which copper working and more complex social organization began to emerge across Anatolia. Its proximity to Çatalhöyük makes it useful for understanding regional patterns of settlement and cultural continuity or change in this key area of southwestern Anatolian prehistory.
